# Develop brand voice → generate a BrandGPT system prompt
Rico is the operations VA for a brand owner launching a new sustainable-apparel line (ASIN B0APP0002 — an organic-cotton everyday tee). It's a fresh launch with **no reviews yet**. The owner said "give our brand a real voice and set me up a BrandGPT my whole team can use so nothing sounds off-brand." Rico has never done brand-voice work, doesn't know what "brand voice," "distinctiveness," or a "voice archetype" means, has plenty of time, and wants to learn the *why* and hand over something clean.

**Rico (User):** Hi! My owner is launching a new sustainable clothing line — organic-cotton tees, ASIN B0APP0002. It's brand new, we have zero reviews so far. She wants me to "give the brand a real voice" and then set up a BrandGPT so the team writes the same way. I've honestly never done brand voice. I don't even know what "brand voice" means exactly. I've got plenty of time — can you teach me step by step and help me hand her something clean?

**Coach:** Perfect task to learn on, and time is the asset we want — we'll go in order so the pieces hold together. Let me define the term first, then I'll show you the plan.

**What "brand voice" means:** the book is clear that it's *more than just words.* Voice is *"the essence of how it communicates, the personality it embodies, and the connection it builds with its audience."* The big mindset shift: voice is **not a copywriting task — it's a relationship-builder.** Its whole job is *establishing trust, evoking emotion, and ensuring consistency* across every customer interaction — which is exactly your owner's worry that the team sounds different everywhere.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/00-brand-voice-overview.md⟧

Here's our plan for the session (we have time, so we'll do it thoroughly):
1. **Pick the one core emotion** the brand will own (I'll pull a few "Signature" options so you don't guess).
2. **Run that voice through the four IDEA pillars** — Insight-Driven, Distinctive, Empathetic, Authentic — I'll define each before we apply it.
3. **Nail it down to the four elements** — Tone, Language, Design, Imagery — the consistency checklist.
4. **Export a paste-ready BrandGPT system prompt** with compliance guardrails baked in.

Sound good? First question so we ground it in your real brand, not theory: in one or two sentences, what does this tee actually stand for — why organic cotton, who's it for?

**Rico (User):** Got it, voice is the personality, not just the words. Okay — it's an everyday tee, GOTS-certified organic cotton, made in a fair-wage factory. It's for people who want to dress more sustainably but are tired of "eco" clothing that's scratchy, baggy, or preachy. The owner always says "sustainable shouldn't mean a sacrifice." Does that help?

**Coach:** That helps a lot — you just handed me the seed of the whole voice ("sustainable without the sacrifice," and a customer who's tired of preachy eco-brands). Let me pull a few **Signature options** so you can *pick* the core emotion instead of guessing one.

Quick definition first so the tool's output makes sense: a **Signature** here is the single core emotion the brand commits to own — the book's rule is that a great voice picks *one* emotion and then makes *every* element reinforce it.   ⟦tool: generate_signature⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/03-aligning-voice-with-emotional-triggers.md⟧ ⟦iv-os: read-canon (get_positioning / get_brand_voice — empty for a net-new launch, so we build it)⟧

**Coach:** Heads-up on a real constraint: the Signature tool can read from your saved conversation, your avatar fields, OR your reviews — and it *declines gracefully if all three are empty* rather than inventing. You have **no reviews yet**, so I'm feeding it the brand facts you just gave me (the `conversation[]` path) — that's enough for it to generate options, and we'll note "no review-mined language yet" as a gap to fill later.   ⟦tool: generate_signature {avatar_id: "avatar_B0APP0002", conversation: ["GOTS organic cotton everyday tee", "fair-wage factory", "for sustainability-minded buyers tired of scratchy/baggy/preachy eco clothing", "sustainable shouldn't mean a sacrifice"]}⟧ → **3 distinct Signature options**, each a different core emotion you could own:

- **Signature 1 — "Comfort with a clear conscience."** Core emotion: **ease / relief.** Owns the no-sacrifice promise — soft, everyday, guilt-free.
- **Signature 2 — "Quietly doing better."** Core emotion: **understated pride.** Owns sustainability *without* the preachiness the buyer is sick of.
- **Signature 3 — "Made to be worn, not worshipped."** Core emotion: **honest practicality.** Owns the "real clothes, real values" angle.

Here's how I'd read these for *your* brand — and then you choose, because owning the choice is the point: 1 and 2 both fit your buyer ("no sacrifice" + "tired of preachy"). My suggestion is a **fusion of 1 + 2 — "Comfortable, quietly better."** Want to go with that, pick a different one, or tweak the wording?

**Rico (User):** Oh, that's clever — option 2 nails the "not preachy" thing my owner keeps complaining about. Let's do your fusion, "Comfortable, quietly better." So now we run it through the four pillars? Can you define each one as we go? I don't know what "Insight-Driven" or "Distinctive" mean yet.

**Coach:** Yes — I'll **define each pillar, give the book's rule, then fill in YOUR brand**, one at a time so it sticks. Locking your Signature as `signature_B0APP0002 = "Comfortable, quietly better."` Let me bring it into the working session so the spec is built on it.   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/00-brand-voice-overview.md⟧

**Coach:** ⟦tool: run_conversation {message: "<build the four-pillar voice spec for B0APP0002 from signature 'Comfortable, quietly better'>", avatar_context: "avatar_B0APP0002", asin: "B0APP0002"}⟧

**Pillar 1 — Insight-Driven.** *Definition:* build the voice around **how the customer wants to be spoken to**, not what the brand wants to say. The book's rule: *"an insight-driven voice reflects how customers want to be spoken to"* — you start from the customer's psychology and **mirror their exact words and tone**.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/01-voice-through-the-four-pillars/00-insight-driven-voice.md⟧

*Important for you:* the book says to pull real language from **reviews, support tickets, and social comments.** You have **no reviews yet** — so here's the work-around (write this down): until reviews come in, mine the language from (a) competitor reviews for similar organic tees, (b) the owner's DMs / customer emails, and (c) how your target buyer talks in sustainability forums. *Your brand, filled (provisional):* mirror words like "soft," "actually fits," "not scratchy," "not preachy" — **not** brand-speak like "eco-conscious apparel solution." We'll mark this pillar **"refresh once real reviews land."**

Quick check before I go on — can you tell me, in your own words, why we can't just write in the brand's favorite words here?

**Rico (User):** Because the voice should sound like how the *customer* talks, not how we talk — so the copy feels familiar to them. And right now we have to borrow that language from competitor reviews and forums since we don't have our own reviews yet. Right?

**Coach:** Exactly right — you've got the whole pillar. That's the insight: familiarity and trust come from mirroring *their* language, and you've correctly spotted that your job for now is to *source* that language elsewhere. Onward.

**Pillar 2 — Distinctive.** *Definition:* make the voice **instantly recognizable** so the brand becomes more than a product. The book gives a two-step method:
1. **Name 2–3 personality traits and pick a "voice archetype."** *Definition of archetype:* a recognizable personality the brand commits to. The book's three on the spectrum: **authoritative/confident, playful/witty, or warm/conversational.**
2. **Audit competitor voices and find the gap nobody occupies.**
Then **infuse your UVP into the voice** — *"if a brand champions simplicity, its messaging should be clear and uncluttered."*   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/01-voice-through-the-four-pillars/01-distinctive-voice.md⟧

*Your brand, filled:* traits = **warm, plain-spoken, quietly confident**; archetype = **warm + conversational**. The competitor gap: most sustainable-apparel voices are either *preachy/activist* or *clinical/eco-jargon* — so **the open gap is "warm and un-preachy."** Your UVP ("sustainable without the sacrifice") infused into the voice = clear, comfortable, never guilt-trippy.

**Rico (User):** That makes sense, and "warm + conversational" definitely feels like the gap since everyone else is preachy. Can you give me an *example* of what an archetype sounds like so I really get it? Like a real brand?

**Coach:** Great ask — and the book hands us exemplars. Think of it as a **tone spectrum** (I'll define Tone fully in step 3, but here's the preview so archetype clicks):

| Brand | Archetype / tone |
|---|---|
| **Rolex** | authoritative & sophisticated |
| **Red Bull** | energetic & dynamic |
| **Innocent Drinks** | friendly & playful |

Your "warm + conversational" sits nearest **Innocent Drinks** — friendly, human, a little playful — *but* anchored by honesty (because you're making a sustainability claim). So: Innocent's warmth, not its silliness.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/02-the-four-elements-of-voice/01-tone-of-voice.md⟧

**Pillar 3 — Empathetic.** *Definition:* make the voice feel **human** by speaking to the customer's reality **before** selling. The book's rule: *"acknowledge customer pain points before presenting solutions."* And there are **three validation modes** to pick from — **encouragement, reassurance, or shared ambition.**   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/01-voice-through-the-four-pillars/02-empathetic-voice.md⟧

*Your brand, filled:* the pain to name first = "I want to shop sustainably but eco-clothes have let me down (scratchy, shapeless, preachy)." Best validation mode here = **shared ambition** ("you want to do better *and* feel good in your clothes — so do we"). And the book is strict on coverage: this same empathetic voice must show up in **social posts, emails, ads, AND customer service** — not just the product page.

Quick check: of the three validation modes — encouragement, reassurance, shared ambition — why did I pick "shared ambition" for this buyer instead of "reassurance"?

**Rico (User):** Hmm… because this buyer isn't scared or anxious, they *want* to do something good — they're ambitious about it — so we join them in that goal rather than calming a fear? Reassurance would be more for someone nervous about a risk.

**Coach:** Spot on — that's exactly the judgment call. Reassurance is for a fearful buyer; shared ambition is for a buyer reaching toward a goal, which is yours. You're picking the right mode for the moment, which is the whole skill.

**Pillar 4 — Authentic.** *Definition:* keep the voice **consistent, transparent, and tied to the brand's mission** so customers never sense a sales-y facade. The book's rule has two filters: **(1) strip exaggerated claims and industry jargon — replace with clarity and honesty; (2) confirm the voice traces back to the mission.** Then test it across an ad, the website, and a support reply — *no "sales mode" switch anywhere.* This pillar matters double for you because **sustainability claims get scrutinized** — overclaiming reads as greenwashing.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/01-voice-through-the-four-pillars/03-authentic-voice.md⟧

*Your brand, filled — a starter "do-not list":* no "save the planet" / "100% guilt-free" / "the most sustainable tee ever"; no vague jargon ("eco-conscious lifestyle solution"). Say only what's **provable** (GOTS-certified, fair-wage factory). That's authentic *and* it keeps you out of greenwashing trouble.

**Rico (User):** Okay, I think I've got all four pillars now. You said next is the "four elements" — tone, language, design, imagery. Can you define those and fill them in too? Especially language — I have no idea how to write a tagline.

**Coach:** Yes. First the **definition of the four elements as a set**, then each box filled. *Definition:* the four elements are the **consistency checklist** — when **tone, language, design, and imagery all align, they create one consistent brand personality** that strengthens relationships and *influences purchasing decisions.* A mismatch anywhere (bold copy on a timid visual) breaks it.   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/02-the-four-elements-of-voice/00-cohesive-voice-overview.md⟧

Here's the reference card — what each element carries:

| Element | What it carries | Your brand, filled |
|---|---|---|
| **Tone** | the emotional signature — *how* the brand speaks | **warm, plain-spoken, honest** (Innocent-warmth, anchored by honesty) |
| **Language** | the distinctive expression — taglines & copy | feature → identity (see worked example below) |
| **Design** | the visual language — color, typography, layout | natural/earthy palette + clean layout (see below) |
| **Imagery** | the visual reinforcement of the emotion | real people in real life, softness you can almost feel |

**Tone — worked detail.** Name it in 2–3 adjectives (done: warm, plain-spoken, honest). The book's lesson from **TAG Heuer**: a tone is only believable when it's **backed by real proof** — TAG Heuer's bold tone is earned by real F1 / elite-athlete partnerships. *Your proof that earns the warmth:* GOTS certification + the fair-wage factory. State the proof so the tone feels earned, not claimed.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/02-the-four-elements-of-voice/01-tone-of-voice.md⟧

**Language — worked example (your tagline lesson).** The book's rule: *stop feature-listing* — translate a **function into an emotional outcome**, the way TAG Heuer's **"Don't Crack Under Pressure"** turns a watch into *resilience.* Use active, aspirational language about **who the customer wants to become.** Watch me convert yours, then you try one:
  - ❌ "GOTS-certified 100% organic cotton tee" → ✅ **"Soft enough to live in. Honest enough to feel good about."**
  - ❌ "Sustainably made everyday basics" → ✅ **"Do better without giving anything up."**

Your turn (you have time — this is the exercise): take the feature *"made in a fair-wage factory"* and turn it into an identity line. Don't list the fact — say what it lets the customer *be.*   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/02-the-four-elements-of-voice/02-language.md⟧

**Rico (User):** Let me try… "Clothes you can feel good in — and good about." Is that the idea? It's saying you feel good wearing it AND good about how it's made, without listing "fair-wage factory."

**Coach:** That's a genuinely good line — you translated the *function* (fair-wage factory) into the *emotional outcome* (feeling good about your choice) and made it about who they get to be. That's exactly the move "Don't Crack Under Pressure" makes. Keep it.

**Design — worked detail.** *Definition:* design is the **visual extension of voice** — every color, typeface, layout carries a message. Use **color psychology deliberately**. The book's anchors:

| Color | Evokes |
|---|---|
| **Red** | energy, passion, urgency |
| **Green** | prestige and excellence |
| **Black & white** | contrast and sophistication (precision) |

*Your brand, filled:* lean into **earthy naturals + green** (green carries prestige/excellence and reads "natural/sustainable" — fits you), with **black-and-white for clean, honest contrast**; **avoid red** (urgency fights your calm, no-pressure feel). Clean, uncluttered layout so the product and the proof stay the focus — and the visual DNA must read the same on the PDP, an ad, and packaging.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/02-the-four-elements-of-voice/03-design-visual-language.md⟧

**Imagery — worked detail.** Reinforce the **one** emotion: real people wearing the tee in ordinary life, fabric softness you can almost feel — *not* a staged "activist with a placard" or a sterile studio shot. Imagery is the visual reinforcement of "comfortable, quietly better."   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/02-the-four-elements-of-voice/00-cohesive-voice-overview.md⟧

**Cohesion gut-check (the closing test).** The book's final rule: when tone, language, design, and imagery **all reinforce the one core emotion across every touchpoint**, the experience feels intuitive and the brand becomes *a symbol of aspiration, identity, and belonging — not just a product.* Read down your four boxes: do they all point at "comfortable, quietly better"? They do — warm tone, no-sacrifice taglines, earthy/calm design, real-life imagery. That's a cohesive voice.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/02-brand-voice/03-aligning-voice-with-emotional-triggers.md⟧

**Rico (User):** This is so much clearer than I expected. Okay — last part: the BrandGPT system prompt. I genuinely don't know how to write one. Can you give me a template I can fill in, and explain what each section is for? And the owner is nervous about sustainability claims, so it has to keep the team from overclaiming.

**Coach:** Yes — and good news, the book gives us a **complete reusable template** so you're filling in a scaffold, not writing from a blank page. Two things to understand first (this is the "why"):

1. **The worksheet is the gate.** Before training any AI, the book says all your brand inputs go into one **IDEA Brand Canvas Worksheet** — and that worksheet is *what you upload* alongside the prompt. The reason: a **thin or improvised brief produces generic AI output later.** A complete worksheet is what makes the AI sound Insight-Driven, Distinctive, Empathetic, and Authentic — the **"four AI-response guarantees."** Your worksheet = the avatar + this voice spec we just built. (Field still open: real review language — note it.)   ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/00-custom-gpt/01-idea-ai-training-workflow/00-prepare-the-worksheet.md⟧

2. **The prompt has fixed section headings** — keep every heading as a checklist, just swap in your brand. And the book is explicit that the **Compliance & "what NOT to say" block is NOT optional** — it's how the brand keeps the AI honest under pressure. That's exactly your owner's greenwashing worry, so we'll make it strict.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/00-custom-gpt/01-idea-ai-training-workflow/02-system-instructions-template.md⟧

Exporting the spec as a paste-ready BrandGPT system-instructions prompt now, using the book's section scaffold verbatim and re-skinning it to your brand:   ⟦tool: export_strategy_doc {avatar_id: "avatar_B0APP0002", asin: "B0APP0002", section: "brandgpt_system_instructions", format: "markdown"}⟧ → see the **Artifact** below. ⟦iv-os: read-safe-claims (get_safe_claims, B0APP0002 → only "GOTS-certified" / "fair-wage" approved; compliance block grounded, not invented)⟧ ⟦iv-os: write-produced-asset (log_asset → voice spec + BrandGPT prompt, request_ids returned)⟧

**Rico (User):** This is perfect — the headings make it so I can't forget a part, and the "what NOT to say" list is exactly what the owner needs. What do I do with it now?

**Coach:** Two-step hand-off, then I'll give you a checklist so you can do this on the *next* SKU yourself:

1. **Build the worksheet file:** put the locked Signature, the four-pillar spec, and the four-element spec into one IDEA Brand Canvas Worksheet doc (I can format it for you). That's the knowledge file.
2. **In BrandGPT:** paste the prompt into the system-instructions box **and** upload the worksheet as its knowledge file — *they work as a pair.* Then run the **test-and-fine-tune** pass: ask it a few real questions and check it stays on-voice and never overclaims.

And the gap to close: **once you have ~10 real reviews, come back** and refresh the Insight-Driven pillar with your buyers' actual words — that's the one provisional box. Want me to format the worksheet doc and draft 3 test questions for the fine-tune pass next?

### Artifact produced

**Locked Signature (`generate_signature`):** `signature_B0APP0002` = **"Comfortable, quietly better"** — core emotion: ease/relief + understated pride (fusion of options 1 + 2 of 3 generated; built from brand facts via the `conversation[]` path because **no reviews exist yet** — the tool would have declined on empty input).

**Brand-Voice Spec (`run_conversation`, four pillars × four elements):**

| IDEA pillar | Rico's brand (B0APP0002) |
|---|---|
| **Insight-Driven** | mirror buyer words ("soft," "actually fits," "not scratchy," "not preachy"); **PROVISIONAL — no reviews yet**, source language from competitor reviews / owner DMs / sustainability forums until real reviews land |
| **Distinctive** | traits: warm, plain-spoken, quietly confident · archetype: **warm + conversational** (nearest Innocent Drinks) · gap owned: "warm & un-preachy" vs the preachy/clinical category · UVP infused: clear, comfortable, never guilt-trippy |
| **Empathetic** | name the pain first ("eco-clothes let me down — scratchy/shapeless/preachy") · validation mode: **shared ambition** · same voice across social/email/ads/service |
| **Authentic** | do-not list: no "save the planet" / "100% guilt-free" / "most sustainable ever" / eco-jargon · say only the provable (GOTS, fair-wage) · no "sales mode" switch · guards against greenwashing |

| Voice element | Rico's brand (B0APP0002) |
|---|---|
| **Tone** | warm, plain-spoken, honest (Innocent-warmth, **earned by** GOTS + fair-wage proof) |
| **Language** | feature→identity taglines: "Soft enough to live in. Honest enough to feel good about." / "Do better without giving anything up." / (Rico's own) "Clothes you can feel good in — and good about." |
| **Design** | earthy naturals + **green** (prestige/excellence, reads natural) + black-and-white (clean contrast); **avoid red**; uncluttered layout |
| **Imagery** | real people in ordinary life, tactile softness — reinforces "comfortable, quietly better" |

**BrandGPT System-Instructions Prompt (`export_strategy_doc`, section `brandgpt_system_instructions`) — paste-ready, re-skinned from the Ch7 verbatim template:**

```
EverydayBetter Apparel GPT

Role & Purpose:
You are EverydayBetter Apparel's GPT, a brand assistant and AI-powered expert for our
GOTS-certified organic-cotton everyday tee. Your role is to give accurate, warm, on-brand
answers about the product, its comfort and sustainability, and to help customers who want to
dress more sustainably without sacrificing fit, softness, or feeling preached at. You have been
provided with our Avatar 2.0 profile and IDEA Brand Canvas Worksheet to ground every answer in
our strategy.

Guiding Principles:
- Insight-Driven: Mirror how customers actually talk ("soft," "actually fits," "not preachy");
  never use brand-speak like "eco-conscious apparel solution."
- Distinctive: Hold a warm, conversational, quietly-confident voice — the opposite of the preachy
  or clinical tone the rest of sustainable apparel uses.
- Empathetic: Acknowledge that eco-clothing has let people down (scratchy, shapeless, preachy)
  BEFORE recommending; speak with shared ambition — "you want to do better and feel good; so do we."
- Authentic: Be transparent and only make provable claims; never exaggerate or greenwash.

Brand Positioning: EverydayBetter
EverydayBetter makes a GOTS-certified organic-cotton everyday tee, made in a fair-wage factory,
for people who want to dress more sustainably but are tired of eco-clothing that's scratchy,
baggy, or preachy. The Signature is "Comfortable, quietly better": warm, honest, no sacrifice —
sustainability without the lecture.

Target Audience:
- Sustainability-minded buyers who refuse to give up comfort or fit.
- People burned by "eco" clothing that was scratchy, shapeless, or preachy.
- Everyday shoppers who want their values in their basics, quietly.

Core Product Benefits:
- Soft, well-fitting everyday tee — no sacrifice in comfort.
- GOTS-certified organic cotton — a provable sustainability standard.
- Made in a fair-wage factory — values you can feel good about.

Brand Voice & Personality:
- Warm & plain-spoken — conversational and human, never preachy or clinical.
- Honest — every confident line is backed by a real, provable fact (GOTS, fair-wage).
- Quietly confident — values stated plainly, never lectured.
- Taglines: "Soft enough to live in. Honest enough to feel good about." / "Do better without giving anything up."

Compliance & Ethical Guidelines:
- Do not make unverified or exaggerated sustainability claims. Never say "saves the planet,"
  "100% guilt-free," "the most sustainable tee," or any superlative we cannot prove.
- Only use claims in the approved safe-claims set (e.g., "GOTS-certified," "fair-wage factory").
  Never invent or imply certifications, carbon figures, or impact stats that aren't approved.
- If asked for a claim we can't substantiate, say what we CAN prove and decline the rest.

Example of what NOT to say:
"This tee saves the planet and is 100% guilt-free!"
Say instead: "It's made from GOTS-certified organic cotton in a fair-wage factory — a real,
verifiable standard — so you can feel good about it without us overpromising."

How to Handle User Queries:
- Competitor comparison → emphasize the comfort + GOTS + fair-wage facts; never run down competitors.
- Sustainability specifics we can't verify → state only what's certified; offer to point to the
  certification rather than guess.
- Sizing / comfort → answer warmly and honestly; reassure about fit and softness.

Final Instructions:
Follow the IDEA Framework — keep every interaction insight-driven, distinctive, empathetic, and
authentic. Maintain EverydayBetter's warm, quietly-confident voice and the do-not list at all times.
Educate and connect first; convert by being trustworthy and never preachy or overclaiming.
```

**Reusable checklist Rico keeps (run on any future SKU):**
> 1. **Define voice** = personality that builds trust, not just words.
> 2. **Pick ONE core emotion** (generate Signature options; choose, don't guess).
> 3. **Four pillars:** Insight-Driven (mirror customer words — source from reviews/DMs/forums if none yet) · Distinctive (2–3 traits + archetype + own the competitor gap + infuse UVP) · Empathetic (name pain first; pick a validation mode: encouragement/reassurance/shared ambition) · Authentic (strip jargon/exaggeration; only the provable; no sales-mode switch).
> 4. **Four elements:** Tone (2–3 adjectives, backed by proof) · Language (feature→identity tagline) · Design (color psychology; pick a palette that matches the emotion) · Imagery (reinforce the one emotion).
> 5. **Cohesion check:** do all four point at the one emotion?
> 6. **BrandGPT:** fill the template headings; keep the Compliance + "what NOT to say" block; upload the worksheet as the knowledge file; run test-and-fine-tune.

**One next action (offered):** format the IDEA Brand Canvas Worksheet doc + draft 3 fine-tune test questions; and after ~10 real reviews, refresh the (currently provisional) Insight-Driven pillar with buyers' real words.

### Assertions (oracle — machine-checkable)
- [tool-call] `generate_signature` invoked first with `{avatar_id: "avatar_B0APP0002", conversation[]}` (the `conversation[]` path, since no reviews/fields exist) → then `run_conversation` (twice — four-pillar pass, then four-element pass — assembling the voice spec) → then `export_strategy_doc` with `{avatar_id: "avatar_B0APP0002", asin: "B0APP0002", section: "brandgpt_system_instructions", format}` — exactly these three tools, in the J5 voice→BrandGPT order (signature → conversation → export).
- [schema] `generate_signature` returns 3–4 distinct Signature options (each a distinct core emotion) and is shown to *decline gracefully on all-empty input* (conversation/fields/reviews) — here it succeeds via the `conversation[]` path; `run_conversation` returns one assembled Trevor reply per turn (buffered) populating the four-pillar + four-element spec; `export_strategy_doc` returns a section/doc (the BrandGPT system-instructions string).
- [skill-faithful] "voice is more than words — the essence of how it communicates / the personality / a relationship-builder that establishes trust, evokes emotion, ensures consistency" traces to 00-brand-voice-overview.md; "reflect how customers want to be spoken to; mirror their exact words; pull language from reviews/support/social" traces to 00-insight-driven-voice.md; "instantly recognizable; 2–3 personality traits + a voice archetype (authoritative/playful/warm-conversational); audit competitors for the gap; infuse the UVP" traces to 01-distinctive-voice.md; "acknowledge pain before solutions; validation modes = encouragement/reassurance/shared ambition; voice across every touchpoint" traces to 02-empathetic-voice.md; "consistent/transparent/aligned-to-mission; strip exaggeration + jargon; no sales-mode switch" traces to 03-authentic-voice.md; the four elements as the consistency checklist (tone/language/design/imagery → one personality) trace to 00-cohesive-voice-overview.md; the Rolex/Red Bull/Innocent tone spectrum + "tone must be backed by proof (TAG Heuer)" trace to 01-tone-of-voice.md; "translate function into emotional outcome like 'Don't Crack Under Pressure'; active/aspirational language about who they become" traces to 02-language.md; color psychology (red = energy/urgency, green = prestige/excellence, black-and-white = contrast/sophistication) + "design is the visual extension of voice" trace to 03-design-visual-language.md; "commit to one core emotion across every touchpoint → aspiration/identity/belonging" traces to 03-aligning-voice-with-emotional-triggers.md; "the worksheet is the gate; a thin brief yields generic AI output; the four AI-response guarantees" traces to 00-prepare-the-worksheet.md; the BrandGPT prompt's section scaffold (Role & Purpose → Guiding Principles mapped to the 4 IDEA pillars → Brand Positioning → Target Audience → Core Product Benefits → Brand Voice & Personality → Compliance with a "what NOT to say" bad/good pair → How to Handle Queries → Final Instructions) and "the compliance block is NOT optional" trace verbatim to 02-system-instructions-template.md (the MyBrand Supplements example re-skinned).
- [persona-adapt:P2] Coach **defined every term before applying it** (brand voice, Signature, each of the four pillars, "voice archetype," the four elements, "worksheet"); gave a **worked example per concept** (the Rolex/Red Bull/Innocent archetype exemplars, the TAG Heuer proof + "Don't Crack Under Pressure" tagline conversion, the color-psychology table, the bad/good compliance pair); delivered the spec **step-by-step with reference tables, fill-in boxes, and a reusable 6-step checklist**; ran **understanding checks** ("why can't we just use the brand's words?", "why shared ambition not reassurance?") and used the **abundant time for a multi-step exercise** (Rico wrote his own identity tagline "Clothes you can feel good in — and good about" and reasoned out the validation-mode choice himself); patient/encouraging; ended on an **offered next guided exercise** (format the worksheet + draft fine-tune questions). Teaching + checklist both present — visibly the scaffolded delivery vs. the P1 compress-and-draft-it-all run.
- [artifact] All three deliverables present: a locked Signature (`signature_B0APP0002`), a brand-voice spec (four IDEA pillars × four elements, filled to Rico's brand, with the Insight-Driven pillar explicitly flagged PROVISIONAL for the no-reviews launch), and a paste-ready BrandGPT system-instructions prompt with the compliance/"what NOT to say" block — plus a reusable checklist.
- [safety] No cross-tenant data; all inputs are the owner's own brand facts (avatar id, ASIN B0APP0002, GOTS/fair-wage, "no sacrifice"/"not preachy") or tool outputs; **no reviews are invented** — the absence of reviews is handled explicitly (Signature built from `conversation[]`, Insight-Driven pillar marked provisional, language-sourcing work-around given) rather than fabricating customer quotes; IV-OS canon/safe-claims reads and asset writes shown only as ⟦iv-os:⟧ capability tags, never invented as concrete records; the BrandGPT compliance block is grounded in the IV-OS safe-claims set (only approved claims like "GOTS-certified"/"fair-wage," explicit "do not invent certifications/carbon figures/impact stats"), and the "what NOT to say" pair bars greenwashing superlatives; no fabricated stats or claims beyond the cited skills.
